Three consecutive mornings saw temperatures in the mid 20s for the Central and Southern San Joaquin Valley agricultural districts.  Hanford dropped to 24 degrees with several other locations showing similar numbers in the center portion of the valley and the mid to upper 20s in the citrus belts.  At the same time and a bit infrequent, fog formed and roads iced with the combination of low temperatures and available moisture from dense fog.

Winds flowing around a strong upper-level high pressure area over  the southern United States, set the stage for several days of mountain and desert thunderstorms. The activity was particularly widespread on the second, with lightning and wind damage reported from the mountains of Riverside County, to the Victor Valley, in San Bernardino County.  Strong winds knocked out power in many areas, and fires ignited by lightning kept fire fighters busy in San Bernardino and Hemet.  One fire burned 5 acres north of Lake Arrowhead, in the San Bernardino Mountains, before it was brought under control.  Pine Cove, in the San Jacinto Mountains, recorded 3.7 inches of rain in just one and one half hours.  Apple Valley  was particularly hard hit, as wind gusts clocked at 62 mph felled trees up to a foot in diameter, downed power lines, damaged a mobile home, and reduced visibilities to around 1/4 mile .  Heavy rain produced local street flooding, and forced the closure of Highway 18 for a time.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind, aided by terrain channeling up a mountain side, lifted a patio deck subsequently pulling the roof off from a house southwest of Stallion Springs.  The damage was very localized, but totally demolishing one house.  Small hail occurred with the thunderstorm passage as reported by a couple of witnesses.

Very small F0 tornado with damage to small rural structures and fencing.  Path width by witness at 10-15 yards.  Tornado accompanying convective band sweeping southwest to northeast through east-Central San Joaquin Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture over the Southwest United States by the late afternoon/early evening of Wednesday, September 3rd, supported thunderstorm activity over the desert portions of Kern County and the northern Kern County Mountains.  One particularly large cell produced heavy rain reaching 4.50 inches in a little over an hour and resulted in 28,000 CFS of flash flood flow down Red Rock Creek through Red Rock Canyon State Park damaging several bridges along State Highway 14.  While not reaching severe wind threshold levels...the Cantil thunderstorm produced a gusty front of 40 knots over the California City Airport 12 miles away at 17:30 PST.  No damage was reported.  An additional gust front from the Mojave area thunderstorm produced a gust front of 42 knots over the Mojave Airport at 18:00 PST.  Again, no damage was reported from this cell due to its winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A  thunderstorm measured at 69 dBz moved from southwest to northeast up the El Paso Mountains in East Kern County on the evening of Wednesday, Sept 3rd.  Reports indicated that 4.50" of rain fell from this storm in a little over an hour's time.  The resulting flash flood through Red Rock Canyon State Park brought 28,000 CFS down through the park, across State Highway 14 and the Redrock-Randsburg Road, and on into Koehn Dry Lake.  Nearly 100 motorists were stranded by the flooding with 4 cars swept into the water.  Reports indicated that a 12-foot wall of water swept over Highway 14 at 7:10 PM PDT and subsequently over Redrock-Randsburg Road at 7:17 PM PDT.  The number of injuries was estimated based on initial reports...no fatalities.
Up to 5 inches of snow fell at the 4000 foot level with up to 8 inches above 5000 feet.
The weather observer at Fort Irwin reported a 58 mph wind gust along with pea-sized hail from a nearby thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure trough aloft spawned funnel clouds and a tornado over coastal areas of central Orange County.  Although no thunder or lightning was detected, convective showers were observed in the area.  The tornado touched down near the Irvine Spectrum Center and travelled about a quarter of a mile  on the ground, ripping away a chain-link fence at a construction site, which landed on 30 cars a few hundred yards away.  Four cars were reportedly picked up and moved a few feet, and wooden beams were tossed about like toothpicks.  No injuries were reported, but ten cars were damaged by flying debris and a shuttle shelter was destroyed.  As the tornado lifted, another funnel developed and lasted for several minutes.

A slow moving low pressure center off the coast, spawned late night showers and thunderstorms.  The showers continuously developed off the coast, and  moved inland over central portions of Orange County, starting before daybreak on the sixth, and lasting until the early morning hours on the seventh.  Rainfall generally ranged from 4 to 8 inches  across the area, resulting in widespread flooding.  One observer in Mission Viejo reported 10 inches of rain from this event.   The hardest hit area was the coastal plain southwest of the Santa Ana Mountains.  It was the heaviest rain recorded in at least 70 years.  Collapsing hillsides, and raging water, triggered flooding that forced scores of people to flee their homes, while others climbed atop stalled automobiles to escape the rising waters.   A Local State of Emergency was declared, as mud and debris flows damaged hundreds of homes and businesses, and halted transportation.  Storm debris littered the coastline.  Some areas, like the Rhine Channel in Newport Bay, were clogged to the point where they resembled a landfill.  Total damage estimates for the county included $5.6 million to private homes and businesses, and over $12 million to public facilities, and for debris clean-up.  Some heavy rain did spill over the Santa Ana Mountains, resulting in flooding in the community of El Cerrito, in western Riverside County.  Over 4 inches of rain caused moderate to heavy damage to 8 homes and and minor damage to 10 others.  Farther north in the Victor Valley of San Bernardino County, heavy rains caused local flooding in many communities.  Heavy downpours in Adelanto shortly after midnight, flooded an apartment complex, forcing residents to evacuate, and sent a river of mud and water a foot deep running down some streets.  During the evening of the sixth, a pair of teenage lobster fisherman were caught in a storm a mile west of the beach at North Island Naval Air Station.  Swells more than 6 feet high capsized their 18-foot skiff.  They were rescued several hours later with cuts and bruises.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter observed a funnel cloud which developed just offshore of Del Mar and moved inland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest swell...from a powerful storm in the Gulf of Alaska...combined with southerly swell from Hurricane Nora to produce heavy surf across the coast of Southern California. On average...the surf was 5 to 8 feet with local sets to 14 feet. Minor coastal flooding was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Unstable conditions in the wake of a cool front helped to spawn a waterspout off of Newport Beach.  The waterspout came ashore at the Newport Pier and quickly dissipated over western Costa Mesa, causing minor power outages.  Little damage, and no injuries were reported, although one fisherman was blown from one end of the pier to another.  Winds were estimated at 60 to 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains were a contributing factor in 11 motor vehicle accidents within a 1/4-mile stretch of Interstate 80 in Solano County.
The Stockton Metropolitan Airport tower observed this funnel cloud.

A Pacific storm brought more rain and snow to Southern California. The storm dropped snow levels down to around 5000 feet. Some mountain locations received up to 5 inches of snowfall. At lower elveations, moderate to heavy rain fell. Rainfall totals ranged from 0.50 to 1.50 inches at the coast...up to 2.50 inches in the mountains.

Roof damage to a large, rural warehouse.  No other damage reported in the agricultural area.  1/2 to 3/4 of 16,000 sq.ft. structure, including two evaporative coolers, were damaged by the gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm affected the Southern Sierra Nevada primarily over a 2-day period beginning in the early morning hours of the 26th and continuing into the 27th.  Mammoth Mountain on the eastern border of Madera County already received 3-5 feet of new snow by the early morning of the 27th.  Rainfall amounts on the west side of the mountains ranged from 2 to 3 inches at elevations between 2500 to 4000 feet...and 3 inches plus from 4000 foot up to the initial snow levels of 5000 feet. Snow levels eventually lowered to just below 4000 feet as the storm passed.

Strong surface high pressure over the western United States forced gusty offshore flow from passes to the coast.  Strong upper-atmospheric flow augmented the surface pressure gradients, resulting in particularly damaging Santa Ana  wind conditions, especially during the late night and morning hours.  The winds toppled hundreds of trees, damaged scores of homes and vehicles, blew  big-rigs off the road, raised clouds of choking dust and sand, restricting visibility, and snapped power poles and lines, sending tens of thousands of customers into darkness.  A wind gust of 96 mph was clocked by the Highway Patrol near Pine Valley, in San Diego County at 8:15 am on the twelfth. A truck window shattered here, and two big-rigs were blown over.  Two construction workers were killed, one in Riverside, when a wind-blown panel knocked him off a water tower, and another in Irvine, when he was struck  in the head by a piece of wind-blown plywood.  A roof was blown off a mobile home, and two house fires were ignited by wind-blown power lines in Santa Ana.  Even Fashion Island's 112-foot Christmas tree was stripped of its world's-largest-tree title, when a gust sheared 30 feet off the top.  In the mountain communities of Riverside County, falling trees damaged homes and power poles, and closed some roads.  A fish farm in Sun City reported in excess of $1 million in structural damages.  Some 5.4 million pounds of avocados were blown off the trees, of which 25% were not recovered.  In Coronado, 4 boats were destroyed and another 4 boats and 15 docks were damaged, when boats broke loose from their moorings in gale-force winds.  Farther north, more than 20 miles off  the mainland, 6 boats were driven ashore, and others sank at their moorings in Avalon Harbor, on the east side of Catalina Island.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms developed across the San Gabriel Mountains and the San Gabriel Valley. Observations reported winds gusting up to 74 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm brought heavy rain...thunderstorms and snow to Southern California. With this storm, snow levels dropped to around 5000 feet. Snow accumulations up to 6 inches were reported in the mountains. At lower elevations, heavy rain and small hail fell. Rainfall totals ranged from 0.50 to 1.50 inches across the coast...up to 4.00 inches in the mountains. The heavy rain produced numerous street flooding. In Alhambra, 5 high school students were swept down a wash by storm runoff. Three students died while two others survived.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow fall:  Crowley Lake 12 inches; Mammoth Mountain Ski Area 2-4 feet; Bodie State Park 12 inches.

CZ_NAME: E NEVADA/E PLACER/E EL DORADO/NE ALPINE
EVENT_NARRATIVE: 24 hour Storm total:  Truckee 8-10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure trough aloft spawned funnel clouds and a tornado over coastal areas of central Orange County.  Although no thunder or lightning was detected, convective showers were observed in the area.  The tornado touched down near the Irvine Spectrum Center and travelled about a quarter of a mile  on the ground, ripping away a chain-link fence at a construction site, which landed on 30 cars a few hundred yards away.  Four cars were reportedly picked up and moved a few feet, and wooden beams were tossed about like toothpicks.  No injuries were reported, but ten cars were damaged by flying debris and a shuttle shelter was destroyed.  As the tornado lifted, another funnel developed and lasted for several minutes.

Strong winds developed ahead of a surface cold front and deep, upper low pressure center off the California coast.  The winds were particularly strong in the Victor Valley, downing trees and power lines, and causing areas of heavy blowing dust and sand.  More than 3000 electric customers lost power for about two hours, including the Cobalt Middle School, where students were sent home early because of the inability to safely move between classrooms.  A 3000 square foot surface of asphalt roofing was ripped off a business in Hesperia, which resulted in minor water damage.   In San Diego County, the winds whipped an awning onto a woman shopping at a farmers market in Carlsbad.  The 71 year-old woman suffered head injuries and was treated and released from a local hospital.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of  El Nino conditions in the Eastern Pacific, a large northwest swell from a storm in the Gulf of Alaska, Hurricane Nora, and high tides, sent 5 to 8 foot waves past the shore, flooding a 14 block stretch of Seal Beach.  Most houses escaped major flooding, as water rose to porches and front doors, but stopped at sandbags placed just inside.  Quick response by city crews helped keep damage to a minimum.  Minor damage was confined to three homes, and a few garages  were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain bands associated with the western fringes of Tropical Storm Nora dropped unseasonable rains throughout much of Southern California.  For many areas it was the first significant rain since April.  Desert areas picked up from 1.5 to 3.0 inches, mountains from 2 to 4 , coastal valleys between 1 and 2, and coasts, under one inch.  Minor urban flooding was reported in many areas, with low-lying roads and intersections under water.  Several highways in the Coachella and Borrego Valleys had to be closed due to high water.  Hundreds of automobile accidents snarled traffic throughout coastal areas of San Diego and Orange Counties.  The heaviest amount of rain reported was 4.7 inches from Mount Laguna, in San Diego County.

Tropical Storm Nora made an impact upon Southern California. Although the center of Nora tracked northward across Baja California and into Arizona...tropical moisture associated with Nora brought significant rainfall to the area. The heaviest rain fell in Los Angeles and Ventura counties with rainfall totals ranging from 0.50 to 0.75 inches at the coast to 1.00 to 1.75 inches in the mountains.

Monsoonal moisture over the Southwest United States by the late afternoon/early evening of Wednesday, September 3rd, supported thunderstorm activity over the desert portions of Kern County and the northern Kern County Mountains.  One particularly large cell produced heavy rain reaching 4.50 inches in a little over an hour and resulted in 28,000 CFS of flash flood flow down Red Rock Creek through Red Rock Canyon State Park damaging several bridges along State Highway 14.  While not reaching severe wind threshold levels...the Cantil thunderstorm produced a gusty front of 40 knots over the California City Airport 12 miles away at 17:30 PST.  No damage was reported.  An additional gust front from the Mojave area thunderstorm produced a gust front of 42 knots over the Mojave Airport at 18:00 PST.  Again, no damage was reported from this cell due to its winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm passage through the East Lake Isabella area of Kern County brought enough rainfall to generage water flow over State Highway 178 at Onyx from Scodie Creek.  Hail was also reported with this thunderstorm up to 1/2 inch in diameter.
Up to 8 inches of snow fell over central Trinity County above 4000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure, building into the Great Basin, produced strong Santa Ana winds across Los Angeles and Ventura counties. In the mountains, northeast winds through passes and canyons were 40 to 50 mph with gusts up to 75 mph. In the valleys, below passes and canyons, northeast winds 30 to 40 mph with gusts up to 50 mph were common. No significant damage was reported.

Monsoonal moisture over the Southwest United States supported thunderstorm development over the desert and mountains of Kern County.  Numerous instances of inclement weather were reported with this activity ... and in some cases the severity was estimated.  No injuries were reported in these events.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of this severe thunderstorm was delayed until the next day but hail size was reported by long-time observers at Mountain Mesa (4 miles east of the town of Lake Isabella)

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An F0 tornado developed as a severe thunderstorm was dissipating in rural, west Merced County just south of Los Banos.  Damage was essentially limited to stored grain/fodder for cattle and some fences.  Tornado was reported by several witnesses with its last point 3 miles farther south than its initial touchdown point.  The path was from the NNW to SSE.
24 hour storm totals:  Northstar 20 inches
Storm total:  Mammoth Lakes 18 inches with 10-11 inches in  5 hours.

Apparently very small tornado (F0) or gustnado accompanied a line of convection through the east-Central San Joaquin Valley.  Tornado/gustnado path was from SSE to NNW.  Damage confined to minor roof damage and uprooting small trees in a north Clovis suburb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Localized heavy rain developed over the San Bernardino Mountains in association with a fast-moving cold front and sharp, low pressure trough aloft.  Between 1.5 and 2.0 inches of rain fell on a recently burned area southwest of Running Springs.  Most of the rain fell in less than one hour, loosening debris and soil on Holcomb Hill, which flowed into, and blocked drainage through box culverts.  The result  was a six-foot wall of mud, debris and water flowing over cars, roads, and homes .  In all, 27 homes , an apartment complex, Acquinas High School, and 14 automobiles were damaged.  Also, trees up to two feet in diameter, along with boulders and other debris, blocked and damaged drainage channels and roads.  The flood stranded four runaway teenagers, who had to be rescued by helicopter.  They were treated for minor bruises and hypothermia.  Total estimated losses exceeded  $2.5 million.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving thunderstorms produced torrential rain near Desert Center. Sheriff's officers reported that parts of Kaiser Road just north of Desert Center were flooded. Sections of Kaiser Road and other roads in the area were washed out. Although there were no rainfall reports, radar data estimated a storm total of nearly four inches.

Radar estimates of between 4 and 5 inches of rain fell with re-generating thunderstorms in Joshua Tree National Park. This rainfall flooded sections of Pinto Basin Road north of the Cottonwood entrance of the park. Thus, sections of the Pinto Basin Road were closed.
Power poles were knocked down.
8 inches of new snow at the higher elevations of the Southern Sierra Nevada.

CZ_NAME: E NEVADA/E PLACER/E EL DORADO/NE ALPINE
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Squaw Valley Ski Resort at 8000 feet reported 12 inches of snow overnight (approximately over a 12 hour period).

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO COUNTY VALLEY/THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Santa Ana winds of 30 to 40 mph with frequent gusts over 60 mph developed below the Cajon Pass , through canyons in the Santa Ana Mountains,  and valley areas of  San Bernardino County.  The wind quickly drove an illegal scrap metal burn out of control in an isolated area north of Santiago Canyon.  The fire raced through Orange County's eastern canyons, destroying two buildings, causing voluntary evacuations, closing schools, and torching almost 6000 acres before it was brought under control.   Elsewhere, trees and power lines were blown down in Rialto and Fontana, and a Highway Patrol shed on the Banning/Beaumont city border was destroyed.  Several wind gusts to 80 mph were recorded at Fremont Canyon.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pre-frontal winds downed 10 power poles in the Bear Valley area of the Tehachapi Mountains.  Gusty wind to 40 MPH on the Southern San Joaquin Valley floor downed numerous trees and powerlines in and around Bakersfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three consecutive mornings saw temperatures in the mid 20s for the Central and Southern San Joaquin Valley agricultural districts.  Hanford dropped to 24 degrees with several other locations showing similar numbers in the center portion of the valley and the mid to upper 20s in the citrus belts.  At the same time and a bit infrequent, fog formed and roads iced with the combination of low temperatures and available moisture from dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Note narrative on fog for same time period.

The Barstow Fire Dept. reported winds estimated at 60 to 70 mph.
Three gusts of 90 knots (104 mph) were reported at the Twentynine Palms Airport.

A 20 mile stretch of State Route 62 was closed between Godwin Road and Highway 177 due to flash flooding.  Mud slides and water had washed several vehicles off the road.  The number of injured motorists was unknown.  State route 62 was badly damaged and remained closed until at least 7:00 pm PST.
Several houses were flooded in the Wonder Valley area, just east of Twentynine Palms.
The Thornton Wildfire in the Sequoia National Forest caused by a lightning strike approximately 5 miles southeast of Lake Isabella.  Cost figures unavailable but it burned 150 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a metal vine support in a vineyard near Lodi, injuring three workers.

The winds damaged many trees and even toppled a few, resulting in power outages affecting 3700 San Bernardino County customers.  Wind gusts were clocked at 76 mph through Fremont Canyon, in the Santa Ana Mountains.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An isolated pocket of dense, radiation fog developed near Lake Elsinore.  The wall of fog shrouded the southbound lanes of the I-15, and surprised motorists on an otherwise clear morning.  In the span of 45 minutes, twenty people were injured in a series of seven accidents along a half mile stretch of the freeway north of the Main Street exit.  Twenty-two vehicles and a big-rig were involved, shutting down the southbound lanes for more than five hours.  Most injuries were minor, but two people were hospitalized.

More than 3000 electric customers lost power for about two hours, including the Cobalt Middle School, where students were sent home early because of the inability to safely move between classrooms. EVENT_NARRATIVE: In heavy rain, a car hydroplaned off Interstate-5 near Lebec into a swollen creek adjacent to the road.  Before a rescue could be accomplished one of the vehicle's occupants was swept downstream.

A complex of very heavy thunderstorms developed over Borrego Springs  and the surrounding mountains and deserts of northeastern San Diego County from Ocotillo Wells, to Julian and Ranchita.  Strong winds clocked at 60 mph, downed six power poles at La Casa Del Zorro Resort.  Downed power lines and flooding forced the closure of Borrego Springs Road between Yaqui Pass and Highway 78.   At  least 1600 residences and businesses in the area were without power, and some without running water  for over 14 hours.

Heavy Surf stirred up by Hurricane Linda, sent waves up to 18 feet high thundering into The Wedge at Newport Beach.  Five people were swept off a jetty, and 300 yards out to sea, before being rescued by a passing boater.  Four were injured, one critically, after nearly drowning.

Shots fired in a target range, set off a fire in the San Bernardino National Forest.  Strong winds in the area helped spread the fire that torched 1500 acres before it was brought under control. Fifteen buildings, including 10 homes were destroyed, most in the tiny communities of Frebalda, Smiley Park, and Live Oak, southwest of Running Springs.  Five hundred people were evacuated from their homes for more than a day, and Highways 330 and 18 were closed for an extended period of  time.   Damage to homes and buildings was estimated at $250,000.
